draft.introduction: At Laws 716c, the Athenian Stranger corrects Protagoras’s famous dictum: it is God, not man, who is the measure of all things. This passage became a key text for theistic Platonism and the idea that assimilation to God is the goal of human life.

translation.text: God is the measure of all things, far more so than any man, as they say. He who would be dear to God must become like God as far as possible. The temperate person is dear to God, being like him; the intemperate person is unlike and at variance with him.
translation.original_text: ὁ δὴ θεὸς ἡμῖν πάντων χρημάτων μέτρον ἂν εἴη μάλιστα

translator_notes.note: The injunction to become “like God as far as possible” (ὁμοίωσις θεῷ) became the defining formula of Platonic ethics. The Church Fathers adopted it as a philosophical expression of theosis—the doctrine that humanity’s end is deification.
